title: DDC Controller Panel Wiring
description: Assemble the DDC controller panel wiring components in the correct order from the power source to a fully operational controller.

parts.name: 120VAC circuit breaker at panel disconnect
24VAC control transformer (120V primary to 24V secondary)
24VAC power to DDC controller power terminals
Field sensor wiring terminated at controller I/O terminals
BACnet MS/TP network cable connected to comm port

explanation: DDC panel wiring follows a power-first sequence: line power through a disconnect, transformer to 24VAC, controller power, field wiring, then network. Never energize the controller before all power connections are verified per NEC Article 725 and manufacturer installation guides.

title: Duct Temperature Sensor Installation
description: Assemble the components for installing a duct-mounted temperature sensor in a supply air duct.

parts.name: Drill pilot hole in duct wall at sensor location
Install sensor mounting flange with gasket on duct exterior
Insert averaging sensor element through flange into airstream
Connect shielded sensor cable to sensor head terminal block
Route sensor cable to DDC controller and terminate at AI terminal

explanation: Duct temperature sensor installation requires a sealed penetration (gasket prevents air leaks), proper insertion depth (sensor element must be in the airstream, not against the duct wall), and shielded cable to prevent electrical interference per ASHRAE Guideline 36.

title: Duct Static Pressure Sensor Piping
description: Assemble the components for installing a duct static pressure sensor, from the sensing point in the duct to the controller.

parts.name: Static pressure probe inserted into duct (perpendicular to airflow)
1/4-inch copper or polyethylene tubing from probe to sensor
Differential pressure transducer mounted near the duct
Reference port on transducer open to plenum space (or piped to outdoor reference)
Analog signal wire (0-10VDC) from transducer to controller AI

explanation: Duct static pressure sensing requires a clean probe perpendicular to airflow, leak-free tubing, and a properly referenced transducer. The reference port must sense the same space as the duct serves (typically the plenum above ceiling) for accurate readings per ASHRAE Guideline 36.

title: Control Valve Assembly on Heating Coil
description: Assemble the control valve and associated components on a hot water heating coil from the supply pipe to the return pipe.

parts.name: Supply isolation valve (ball valve)
Two-way modulating control valve with actuator
Heating coil supply and return connections
Return isolation valve with balancing port

positions.label: Position 1: From hot water supply main
Position 4: To hot water return main

explanation: Heating coil control valve assemblies place the control valve on the supply side for proper authority, with isolation valves for service access. The balancing valve on the return allows hydronic balancing during commissioning. Heating valves are set to fail-open (spring return to open) per ASHRAE Guideline 36.

title: BACnet MS/TP Network Trunk
description: Assemble the components for a BACnet MS/TP network trunk connecting multiple DDC controllers.

parts.name: 120-ohm end-of-line termination resistor at first device
Shielded twisted-pair cable daisy-chained from controller to controller
Shield drain wire grounded at one end only (typically at router)
BACnet router connecting MS/TP trunk to BACnet/IP backbone
120-ohm end-of-line termination resistor at last device

explanation: BACnet MS/TP trunks require termination resistors at both ends to prevent signal reflections, daisy-chain (not star) topology, and single-point shield grounding to prevent ground loops. The router bridges the field bus to the building IP network per ASHRAE 135.

title: AHU Controls — Outdoor Air to Discharge
description: Assemble the control points for an AHU in order from the outdoor air intake to the supply fan discharge.

parts.name: Outdoor air temperature/humidity sensor at intake
Outdoor air damper with modulating actuator
Mixed air temperature sensor (downstream of mixing plenum)
Filter differential pressure switch across filter bank
Cooling coil with chilled water control valve
Supply air temperature sensor and duct static pressure sensor at discharge

explanation: AHU control points follow the air path: outdoor sensing at intake, damper control at entry, mixed air sensing after blending, filter monitoring, coil control for conditioning, and discharge sensing for closed-loop control. This sequence is defined by ASHRAE Guideline 36.

title: VAV Box Controller Installation
description: Assemble the components for installing a DDC VAV box controller, from mounting to operational verification.

parts.name: Mount DDC controller on VAV box housing using manufacturer bracket
Wire 24VAC power from transformer to controller power terminals
Connect damper actuator cable to controller output terminal
Connect zone temperature sensor cable to controller input terminal
Connect airflow sensor tubing to controller differential pressure input
Connect BACnet MS/TP trunk cable to controller comm port

explanation: VAV controller installation follows: mount, power, outputs, inputs, then network. Powering the controller before wiring outputs prevents unexpected actuator movement. The airflow sensor connects via pneumatic tubing, not wire, to the controller's built-in differential pressure transducer.

title: Chiller Plant Control Sequence Assembly
description: Assemble the control sequence steps for starting a water-cooled chiller from the plant controller's perspective.

parts.name: Open chiller isolation valves (CHW supply and return)
Start condenser water pump and verify flow
Start primary chilled water pump and verify flow
Start cooling tower fan to establish condenser water temperature
Send chiller enable command and verify compressor starts
Monitor chiller operating parameters (pressures, temps, amps) for stability

explanation: Chiller startup sequence is critical: water flow must be established on BOTH condenser and evaporator sides BEFORE the compressor starts. Starting a chiller without water flow causes evaporator freeze-up or condenser high-pressure lockout per ASHRAE Guideline 36 and chiller manufacturer requirements.

title: Boiler Safety Chain Wiring
description: Assemble the boiler safety chain components in the correct wiring order from the safety circuit power supply to the gas valve.

parts.name: Safety circuit 24VAC power supply (R terminal)
High-limit temperature switch (manual reset, normally closed)
Low-water cutoff switch (manual reset, normally closed)
Flame rollout switch (manual reset, normally closed)
BAS enable relay contact (normally open, closes on call for heat)
Gas valve solenoid coil (energizes to open gas)

explanation: The boiler safety chain is a series circuit: all safety switches must be closed AND the BAS must call for heat before the gas valve can open. If any safety trips, the circuit breaks and the gas valve de-energizes. Safety devices are wired in series BEFORE the BAS enable relay, ensuring safeties cannot be overridden by the BAS per NFPA 85 and ASME CSD-1.

title: Economizer Damper Controls Assembly
description: Assemble the economizer damper control components from outdoor air intake to the AHU mixing plenum.

parts.name: Outdoor air temperature and enthalpy sensor at intake
Outdoor air damper with spring-return modulating actuator
Return air damper with modulating actuator (linked to OA damper)
Mixed air temperature sensor downstream of mixing plenum

explanation: Economizer control requires outdoor sensing to determine free-cooling availability, coordinated outdoor and return dampers for air mixing, and mixed air sensing for closed-loop temperature control. The OA damper must have spring-return to minimum position on power failure per ASHRAE Guideline 36 and 90.1.

title: Occupancy Sensor Installation for DCV
description: Assemble the components for installing a CO2-based occupancy/demand-controlled ventilation sensor in a conference room.

parts.name: Mount CO2 sensor housing on interior wall at 3-6 feet height
Wire sensor signal cable (0-10VDC or BACnet) to DDC controller
Configure CO2 setpoint in controller (typically 800-1000 ppm)
Program DCV sequence: modulate OA damper based on CO2 level
Calibrate sensor against reference CO2 meter in occupied conditions

explanation: CO2-based demand-controlled ventilation reduces energy by matching outdoor air to actual occupancy. The sensor must be at breathing height on an interior wall, properly calibrated, and programmed per ASHRAE 62.1 Section 6.2.7 and ASHRAE 90.1 requirements.

title: Energy Meter Pulse Output to BAS
description: Assemble the components for connecting an electrical energy meter's pulse output to the BAS for energy monitoring.

parts.name: Install current transformers on each phase conductor at the electrical panel
Connect CT secondary wires to the energy meter sensing inputs
Connect meter voltage sensing leads to each phase and neutral
Wire pulse output (dry contact) from meter to DDC controller binary input
Configure controller to accumulate pulses and convert to kWh using meter K-factor
Verify BAS kWh reading matches meter display after 24-hour test period

explanation: Energy metering integration requires proper CT installation (correct ratio and orientation), voltage sensing for power factor calculation, and accurate pulse counting in the BAS. The meter K-factor (pulses per kWh) must be correctly configured. Verification against the meter display confirms accuracy per ASHRAE 90.1 Section 8.

title: CRAC Unit iCOM Controller Integration
description: Assemble the components for integrating a Liebert CRAC unit with the iCOM controller and base building BMS on the SACC project.

parts.name: Mount iCOM controller on CRAC unit and connect to unit sensors
Connect leak detector at drip pan to iCOM alarm input
Wire iCOM to Liebert vNSA network switch for lead/lag coordination
Configure BACnet communication module in iCOM for BMS integration
Map CRAC status points to base building BMS for remote monitoring

explanation: CRAC unit integration on the SACC project follows a local-to-global sequence: first the iCOM controller connects to the unit's local sensors and safety devices, then coordinates with the redundant unit through the vNSA switch, then connects to the building BMS via BACnet. This ensures local safety (leak detection) is operational before enabling network features.

title: Motorized Outside Air Damper with Fan Interlock
description: Assemble the components for installing a motorized outside air damper with evaporator fan interlock on the SACC high-rise TI project.

parts.name: Install motorized damper in outside air duct opening
Mount spring-return actuator on damper shaft
Wire actuator control signal to DDC controller analog output
Wire damper end switch to evaporator fan starter interlock circuit
Wire damper position feedback to DDC controller binary input for BMS monitoring

explanation: The SACC specification requires the outside air damper to be interlocked with the evaporator fan via a hardwired end switch. The spring-return actuator ensures the damper closes on power failure. The end switch in the fan starter circuit prevents the fan from starting until the damper is confirmed open, protecting against operating the fan with a closed damper per IMC Section 403.2.

title: BTU Meter Installation for Base Building BMS
description: Assemble the components for installing a BTU meter on the tenant chilled water connection and integrating it with the base building BMS on the SACC project.

parts.name: Install ultrasonic flow meter on chilled water supply pipe with required straight pipe runs
Install matched temperature sensors in supply and return pipe thermowells
Wire flow meter and temperature sensors to BTU meter calculator/display
Configure BACnet communication module in BTU meter for BMS integration
Map BTU, flow rate, and temperature points to base building BMS with trending enabled
Calibrate meter against reference instruments and document accuracy verification

explanation: BTU metering on the SACC project follows a sense-calculate-integrate-verify sequence. The flow meter requires adequate straight pipe runs for accuracy. Matched temperature sensors ensure accurate delta-T measurement. BACnet integration with the base building BMS enables remote monitoring and trend data collection for tenant energy cost allocation per ASHRAE 90.1 Section 8 and LEED requirements.

title: Firestat and Smoke Detector Safety Chain
description: Assemble the HVAC safety shutdown chain from the firestat and smoke detector to the system shutdown on the SACC high-rise TI project.

parts.name: Firestat sensor element installed in return air duct (set at 125 degrees F)
Duct smoke detector installed in supply air duct
Hardwire both devices in series to HVAC system disconnect relay
Wire smoke detector alarm output to audio/visual indicator at control display panel
Connect status monitoring points to BAS for remote alarm notification

explanation: The SACC safety chain requires both firestat (return air >125 degrees F) and smoke detector (supply air) to be hardwired in series to the HVAC disconnect. This ensures either device can shut down the system independently of BAS software. The audio/visual alarm at the control display panel provides immediate local notification per NFPA 90A. BAS monitoring provides remote notification but is never the primary shutdown mechanism for life-safety functions.

title: Complete TI BAS Integration with Base Building BMS
description: Assemble the integration layers for connecting the SACC tenant improvement BAS on Levels 30-32 to the base building BMS, from field devices through building-wide integration.

parts.name: DDC controllers for VAV zones, CRAC iCOM controllers, and lighting panels on TI network
BACnet gateway/router between TI BAS subnet and base building BMS network
BTU meter BACnet points mapped to base building BMS for energy monitoring
Fire alarm integration — TI smoke/fire devices connected to base building fire alarm panel
Motorized shades and Title 24 lighting controls coordinated with base building schedule
Enhanced LEED commissioning verification of all integrated systems by independent CxA

explanation: High-rise TI BAS integration on the SACC project requires a layered approach: field devices first, then network connectivity to the base building, then energy metering integration, then life-safety coordination, then automation scheduling, and finally independent commissioning verification. Each layer depends on the previous one — you cannot verify BTU meter integration before the BACnet gateway is established, and you cannot commission the integrated system until all layers are connected. Enhanced LEED commissioning by an independent CxA verifies the entire integration meets the Owner's Project Requirements per ASHRAE Guideline 0.
